Shimonakake ritual ({{ill|lt=December 23rd|Q2676|qid=Q2676|1=December 23}}) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Hiraoka Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} {{List of coordinates}} [[File:Hiraoka-jinja, Ninotorii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Ninotorii}}]]'''Hiraoka Shrine'' (Hiraoka Shrine) is located in {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|qid=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Higashiosaka City|Q243863|qid=Q243863|1=Higashiōsaka}}Izumo Icho.{{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinai Shrine|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}), {{ill|lt=Kawachikuni|Q1047150|qid=Q1047150|1=Kawachi Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|1=Ichinomiya}}. {{ill|lt=Modern Shrine Ranking System|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=Old Shrine Ranking|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|lt=Modern Shrine Ranking System|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=Old Shrine Ranking|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|lt=Modern Shrine Ranking System#Government Shrine|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=Kanpei Taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and currently it is {{ill|lt=Beppyō Shrine|Q10898274|qid=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Shrine Head Office|Q908077|qid=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. === Overview === {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}} is located at the western foot of {{ill|lt=Ikoma Mountains|Q11574917|qid=Q11574917|1=Ikoma Mountains}} in eastern Osaka Prefecture, facing west. It is said to have originated from {{ill|lt=mountain worship|Q93697439|qid=Q93697439|1=Mountain worship}} at Kozudake on the back mountain, and is known as the {{ill|lt=guardian deity|Q767655|qid=Q767655|1=Ujigami}} of the Nakatomi clan, whose main deity is {{ill|lt=Amagiya no Mikoto|Q2564508|qid=Q2564508|lt=Amagoyane no Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, the ancestor of {{ill|lt=Nakatomi clan|Q1198447|qid=Q1198447|1=Nakatomi clan}}. The {{ill|lt=Fujiwara clan|Q641628|qid=Q641628|1=Fujiwara clan}}, which separated from the Nakatomi clan, established {{ill|lt=Kasuga Taisha|Q714559|qid=Q714559|1=Kasuga-taisha}} as the {{ill|lt=guardian|Q767655|qid=Q767655|1=Ujigami}}. When it was first built, two of the four deities enshrined were the spirits of our company's Amagoyane-no-Mikoto and Hime-kami, which is why it is also called ``Moto Kasuga.'' After that, as the Fujiwara clan prospered, its reverence increased, and in the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|qid=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, {{ill|lt=Divine Rank|Q11591025|qid=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} reached the highest rank of Shoichii, Order 3rd Class. After {{ill|lt=Middle Ages|Q12554|qid=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}}, it was revered as {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|1=Ichinomiya}} of {{ill|lt=Kawachi Province|Q1047150|qid=Q1047150|1=Kawachi Province}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=UNKNOWN}}, it is one of the most important ancient shrines in the country and is ranked as the highest {{ill|lt=Modern Shrine Ranking System#Government Shrine|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=Kanpei Taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} in the {{ill|lt=Modern Shrine Ranking System|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}.The main shrine is in the {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|qid=Q3193781|lt=Kasuga|1=Kasuga-zukuri}} quadruple-Hiraoka-zukuri style, and is designated as a {{ill|lt=tangible cultural property|Q2901860|qid=Q2901860|1=Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)}} by Higashiosaka City. In addition, many rituals have been passed down to the present day, and among the special rituals, the porridge fortune-telling ritual is an intangible folk cultural property that should be recorded in Osaka Prefecture.|Q11642584|selection It has been selected as an intangible folk cultural property}}, and the Shimenawake Shinto ritual has been designated as an {{ill|lt=folk cultural property#intangible folk cultural property|Q24895900|qid=Q24895900|lt=intangible folk cultural property|1=Folk Cultural Property}} by Higashiosaka City. === Company name === Regarding the company name of Hiraoka Shrine, the following main names can be found in historical sources: * Amagoyane no Mikoto - “{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Koki|Q1041301|qid=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}” * Hiraoka Grand Shrine - “Shoku Nihon Koki” * Hiraoka Kami - "{{ill|lt=The Annals of Japan's Emperor Montoku|Q1052905|qid=Q1052905|1=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku}}" ・"{{ill|lt=The Annals of the Three Japanese Dynasties|Q1052981|qid=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}" Article dated October 21, 865, "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" (Refer to {{Harvnb|Collection of Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials}}). Article dated December 17, 865, "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" (see {{Harvnb|Collection of Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials}}). ・“{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}” Shijisaijou * Hiraoka Tenjiyane no Mikoto - “Nihon Sandai Jiroki” * Hiraokajin - "{{ill|lt=Shinsho Kakuchofusho|Q11502312|qid=Q11502312|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō}}" ・"Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" Article dated September 8, 1st year of Jogan (859) from "Nihon Sandai Jironroku" (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). * Hiraoka Shrine - "Engishiki" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}・Temporary festival * Hiraokasha - "Engishiki" {{ill|lt=Temporary Festival|Q11612499|qid=Q11612499|1=Temporary Festival}}・Shiji Festival 2 As seen above, since {{ill|lt=Ancient times|Q41493|qid=Q41493|1=Ancient history}}, the characters ``Hiraoka'' and ``Hiraoka'' have been used together in the company name.{{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Gods)|2000}} This trend was followed until {{ill|lt=early modern times|Q5308718|qid=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}, and the word ``Hiraoka'' is also recognized on the stone monuments and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=lanterns|1=UNKNOWN}} that remain today {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Gods)|2000}}. === Enshrined God === The following four deities are currently enshrined. [http://www.hiraoka-jinja.org/history/index.html History] (Hiraoka Shrine homepage). . One pillar is enshrined in each of the four main halls (first, second, third, and fourth halls). :* First Hall: {{ill|lt=Amagiya no Mikoto|Q2564508|qid=Q2564508|lt=Amagoyane no Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} (Amenokoyane no Mikoto) - The main deity, the ancestral deity of the Nakatomi clan. :* Second Hall: {{ill|lt=Hime-kami|Q22070227|qid=Q22070227|lt=Hime-gokami|1=Himegami}} (Hime-mikami) - Amagoyane Mikoto. :* Third Hall: {{ill|lt=Katsutsu no Mikoto|Q1107286|qid=Q1107286|lt=Tunetsu no Mikoto|1=Futsunushi}} (Futsunushi no Mikoto, Saishu no Mikoto) - {{ill|lt=Katori Shrine|Q372380|qid=Q372380|1=Katori Jingū}} Enshrined deity. :* Fourth Hall: {{ill|qid=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|qid=Q1502690|qid=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi no Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} (Takemikazuchi no Mikoto, Takemikazuchi no Mikoto) - {{ill|lt=Kashima Shrine|Q706499|qid=Q706499|1=Kashima Jingū}}Enshrined deity.There are currently four deities enshrined, but "{{ill|lt=Shoku Nippon Koki|Q1041301|qid=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}" {{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|qid=Q1075605|lt=Jowa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}}3 ({{ill|lt=836|Q24957|qid=Q24957|1=836}}) Article was the first time I saw it, and in ancient times only the two pillars of Amagoyane-no-Mikoto and Hime-jin were recognized. The first mention of the four deities enshrined in the literature is in "{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jiroku|Q1052981|qid=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}" {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|qid=Q1075635|lt=Jōkan|1=Jōgan}} 7th year ({{ill|lt=865|Q30350|qid=Q30350|1=865}}) "Hiraoka Shrine Shizen" is written {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. {{ill|lt=Extension (era name)|Q1327274|qid=Q1327274|lt=Extension|1=Enchō}} "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|qid=Q30470|1=927}}) The enshrined deity in group="Hara"/> is listed as 4 deities {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine 4 deities (Shikinaisha)|1979}} The {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Honjibutsu|1=UNKNOWN}} of the main deity Amagoyane no Mikoto is {{ill|lt=Jizo Bodhisattva|Q604687|qid=Q604687|1=Kṣitigarbha}}{{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|pp=48-49}}. === About the enshrined deity === [[File:Kasuga-taisha11bs3200.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{Center|{{ill|lt=Kasuga Taisha|Q714559|qid=Q714559|1=Kasuga-taisha}} ({{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|qid=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nara City|Q169134|qid=Q169134|1=Nara (city)}})}}{{Small|Fujiwara clan shrine. At the time of its construction, the spirits of Amagoyane-no-Mikoto and Hime-kami were solicited from Hiraoka Shrine, and they are enshrined in the third and fourth halls, respectively. }}]]The main enshrined deity, {{ill|lt=Amagiya-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|qid=Q2564508|lt='''Amenokoyane-no-Mikoto'' (Amenokoyane)|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, is known as the ancestral deity of the ancient clan ``''{{ill|lt=Nakatomi clan|Q1198447|qid=Q1198447|1=Nakatomi clan}}'' (Nakatomiuji, Nakatomi-ren) and the {{ill|lt=Fujiwara clan|Q641628|qid=Q641628|1=Fujiwara clan}}, which was separated from the Nakatomi clan. In "{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|qid=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}" and "{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|qid=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}," this god appears to be Nakatomi no kami, Nakatomi ren toso, and Nakatomi kamiso. When {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|qid=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}'s {{ill|lt=Amaniwato|Q3231835|qid=Q3231835|lt=Iwatogakure|1=Amano-Iwato}} It is said that he performed religious rituals with Tama no Mikoto}} ({{ill|lt=Ibe clan|Q8189229|qid=Q8189229|1=Inbe clan}} ancestor deity), It is recorded that he followed the god as one of the five gods (Goban-o) at the time of the descent of the descendants of the Tenson (Mikoto) {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. In "{{ill|lt=Newly Selected Names and Names Roku|Q1045362|qid=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}", there are many Nakatomi clans in Kawachi Province, and among them, the Hiraoka clan (Hiraokaren) is said to be our Hosai clan.{{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{Sfn|Hikaru Matsuo|2014}}.{{ill|lt=Hime-no-Kami|Q22070227|qid=Q22070227|1=Himegami}}, along with Amagoyane no Mikoto, is the consort deity of Amagoyane no Mikoto. . The first written reference to the deity being enshrined is in the 3rd year of the Jowa era (836). The specific name of the god is not known in literature, but according to the shrine's legend it is Amemizutamateruhime no mikoto. It should also be noted that in literature, Amagoyane-no-Mikoto and Hime-kami are always described as a pair {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Gods)|2000}}. ``Engishiki'' ``Kasuga Festival Oratory'' ``Engishiki'' Volume 8 (Norito) Kasuga Festival Article. , when the Fujiwara clan founded {{ill|lt=Kasuga Taisha|Q714559|qid=Q714559|1=Kasuga-taisha}} ({{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|qid=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nara City|Q169134|qid=Q169134|1=Nara (city)}}) as a guardian deity, It is said that he enshrined the spirits of Hiraoka Zatennoko Yakone no Mikoto (Amagoyane no Mikoto) and Hime deity {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{Sfn|Matsuo Hikaru|2014}}, and even now, both deities are enshrined in the third and fourth halls of Kasuga Taisha Shrine.The other two enshrined deities, {{ill|lt=Katsutsu no Mikoto|Q1107286|qid=Q1107286|lt=Futunushi|1=Futsunushi}} and {{ill|qid=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|qid=Q1502690|qid=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|1=Takemikazuchi}}, are respectively {{ill|lt=Katori Shrine|Q372380|qid=Q372380|1=Katori Jingū}} ({{jalink|Chiba Prefecture|Q {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}} is the main enshrined deity of 80011}}{{ill|lt=Katori City|Q858357|qid=Q858357|1=Katori, Chiba}}) and {{ill|lt=Kashima Shrine|Q706499|qid=Q706499|1=Kashima Jingū}} ({{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|qid=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kashima City|Q467471|qid=Q467471|1=Kashima, Ibaraki}}). The first written mention of the deity enshrined in our company is the aforementioned Jogan 7th year (865) article . Both deities, like Amagoyane-no-Mikoto and Hime-kami, were offered at the time of the founding of Kasuga Taisha Shrine (Kasuga Taisha Shrine Second Hall and First Hall) {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{Sfn|Hikaru Matsuo|2014}}, At Hiraoka Shrine, as with Kasuga Taisha, both deities are enshrined, and from 2 to 4 deities are enshrined. It is said that it was changed to {{ill|lt=Jingo Keiun|Q1373049|qid=Q1373049|1=Jingo-keiun}}2 ({{ill|lt=768|Q29961|qid=Q29961|1=768}}), which is said to be the founding year of Kasuga Taisha Shrine.The exact year in which Kasugasha was founded is not clear. ``Jingo Keiun 2nd Year'' is based on the shrine diary of Kasuga Taisha Shrine, and is supported by the article dated August 26, 1980 in the ``Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' (``Kasuga Taisha Shrine'', ``National History Dictionary'', Yoshikawa Kobunkan). {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|pp=48-49}}{{Sfn|Hikaru Matsuo|2014}} (in the shrine biography) jalink|Houki|Q18520676}}9 ({{ill|lt=778|Q30452|qid=Q30452|1=778}}). In addition, "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" article dated July 10, 860. There is also a theory that the shrines of Mikafuto no Mikoto and Hikosa Jifutō no Mikoto in Kawachi Province are compared to Takemikatsuchi no Mikoto and Tsunetsu Nushi no Mikoto of our company. In addition to the theory that it is compared to the two shrines of Hiraoka Shrine, there is another theory that it is compared to {{ill|lt=Yuge Shrine (Yao City)|Q11487076|qid=Q11487076|lt=Yuge Shrine|1=Yuge Shrine}} in Wakae District, Kawachi Province. . === History === === Founding === In the shrine biography ("Genyoki" etc.), three years before the enthronement of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|qid=Q200188|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, on the occasion of {{ill|lt=Jinmu Tosei|Q11590444|qid=Q11590444|1=Jimmu's Eastern Expedition}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amatsune no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} is said to have started with the enshrinement of the two gods Amagoyane-no-Mikoto and Hime-kami by imperial order to Kamitsudake on the eastern mountain.{{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{Sfn|Matsuo Hikaru|2014}}. Later, in the first year of {{ill|lt=White Pheasant|Q540118|qid=Q540118|1=Hakuchi (era)}} ({{ill|lt=650|Q30179|qid=Q30179|1=650}}), the Hiraoka Ren (Hiraoka clan) and others moved it from Kozudake to its current location.The Amatsune no Mikoto that appears in the above legend is from the "{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|qid=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}" Pre-Enthronement Period of Emperor Jinmu "Nihon Shoki" Pre-Enthronement Period of Emperor Jimmu October Shintori (5th) article in the year of Kotora. In , he appears to have married Usazuhime, the founder of Tsukushi's Susa Kunizukuri, and is said to be a vassal of Emperor Jimmu and a distant ancestor of the Nakatomi clan. Although the historical reality of the founding tradition is not clear, historical research indicates that the shrine was founded by mountain worship at Kozudake {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. The ritual was carried out by the Hiraoka clan, an ancient clan in the area.{{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}} There is a theory that because the Oka clan and the Nakatomi clan were related, the enshrined deity became Amagoyane no Mikoto, the ancestor of the Nakatomi clan.{{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{Sfn|Hikaru Matsuo|2014}}. In addition, among the place names that appear in "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japanese name extract|1=UNKNOWN}}", this place is compared to Toyoura-go in {{ill|lt=Kawachi-kuni|Q1047150|qid=Q1047150|1=Kawachi Province}}{{ill|lt=Kawachi-gun|Q44611|qid=Q44611|1=Kawachi District, Tochigi}}, but {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine Four Seats (Shikiuchi-sha) |1979 }}{{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Gods)|2000}}, In the neighboring district of Kawachi Province {{ill|lt=Saura District|Q11632569|qid=Q11632569|1=Sasara district}}, there is Hiraoka-go (variant version is Makioka-go), and there is a theory that this place is the origin of Hiraoka Shrine.{{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Gods)|2000}}. === Outline === ==== Ancient ====

In ``Hiraoka God'' at that time, {{ill|lt=Kobe (Private House)|Q11589711|qid=Q11589711|lt=Kobe|1=Kanbe (social class)}} A total of 60 houses, 4 from {{ill|lt=Kawachi Province|Q1047150|qid=Q1047150|1=Kawachi Province}} and 56 from {{ill|lt=Tamba Province|Q954511|qid=Q954511|1=Tanba Province}}, are allocated for {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.In the {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|qid=Q1151168|lt=Kokuhiki|1=Rikkokushi}}, about the {{ill|lt=jinka|Q11591025|qid=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} of Amagoyane-no-Mikoto and Hime-kami in {{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|qid=Q1075605|lt=Jowa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=836|Q24957|qid=Q24957|1=836}}) Senior third grade, third rank, junior fourth rank, upper rank, 6th year of Jowa ({{ill|lt=839|Q23874|qid=Q23874|1=839}}) (Japan)|Q1075611|Tenan}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|qid=Q23897|1=859}}) appears to have been promoted to the rank of Shoichii, 3rd class, and Jusanmi, 6th class {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also, there is an article that adds 24 pieces of cloth to the {{ill|lt=Narihira|Q1324664|qid=Q1324664|1=Saikō}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=856|Q30370|qid=Q30370|1=856}}) , and the 1st year of {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|qid=Q1075635|lt=Jōkan|1=Jōgan}} ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|qid=Q23897|1=859}}) An article submitted to Prayer for Rain, 7th year of Jogan ({{ill|lt=865|Q30350|qid=Q30350|1=865}}), there is an article that gives an example of offerings at the spring and winter festivals in accordance with Kasuga Shrine (Kasuga Taisha Shrine) and Oharano Shrine ({{ill|lt=Oharano Shrine|Q3594020|qid=Q3594020|1=Ōharano Shrine}}) {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.{{ill|lt=Extension (era name)|Q1327274|qid=Q1327274|lt=Extension|1=Enchō}} "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|qid=Q30470|1=927}}) {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}"Engishiki" Volume 9 (Shinmei 1) Kawachi District Kawachi District. In Kawachi Province {{ill|lt=Kawachi District (Osaka Prefecture)|Q11553458|qid=Q11553458|lt=Kawachi District|1=Kawachi district}} there are ``Hiraoka Shrine 4 Namiishindai'' As part of the ``Monthly Aina Niiname'', 4 dais will be listed at {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}, as well as {{ill|lt=Monthly Festival|Q11516161|qid=Q11516161|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri}} and {{jalin k|Ainamesai|Q11581944}} and {{ill|lt=Ninamesai|Q11501518|qid=Q11501518|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}}, it is written on the heihaku that it is kept in {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine 4-za (Shikinaisha)|1979}}. Also, among the special festivals of "Engishiki", the "Ramen God Festival" section of the "Engishiki" volume 3 (temporary festival). In , you can see four shrines of Hiraoka Shrine among the 85 shrines of the Prayer Rain Festival, as well as Meishin Shrine 'Engishiki' Volume 3 (Temporary Festival) Meishin Shrine. shows that there are four shrines at Hiraoka Shrine among the 285 shrines of the Meishin Festival {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also, ``Engishiki'' Shijisai Festival 1st and 2 ``Engishiki'' Volume 1 (Shijisai 1st) February Festival Hiraoka God 4-za Festival Article. "Engishiki" Volume 2 (Shijisai 2) November Festival Hiraokasha Shizajo. In , detailed ritual fees are stipulated {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.According to "{{ill|lt=Honcho Century|Q871866|qid=Q871866|1=Honchō Seiki}}" and "{{ill|lt=Nihon Kiryaku|Q1053542|qid=Q1053542|1=Nihon Kiryaku}}", offerings were often held after that due to wind, rain, and epilepsy. Also, {{ill|lt=Kannin|Q1193951|qid=Q1193951|1=Kannin}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=1017|Q19249|qid=Q19249|1=1017}}) "Sakeiki" October 2nd year of Kannin 1st year (1017). In , Onchi Shrine ({{ill|lt=Onchi Shrine|Q11492258|qid=Q11492258|1=Onji Shrine}}) and our company were chosen from Kawachi Province for the grand dedication of the Emperor's accession to the throne. ==== Medieval/Early modern ==== From the end of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|qid=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, it is said to have been revered as {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|1=Ichinomiya}} of Kawachi Province {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. However, as a historical document showing Ichinomiya status, there is a "annual tribute order for Jingikan shrines" in the first year of {{ill|lt=Eiman|Q1193868|qid=Q1193868|1=Eiman}} ({{ill|lt=1165|Q19676|qid=Q19676|1=1165}}), which includes our company, Onchi Shrine, and Yuge Shrine ({{ill|lt=Yuge Shrine (Yao City)|Q11487076|qid=Q11487076|lt=Yuge Shrine|1=Yuge Shrine}}), but there are no documents related to Hiraoka Shrine that specify Ichinomiya in the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|qid=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}} period. The description can only be seen in ``{{ill|lt=Dainippon Koku Ichinomiya Chronicle|Q11435648|qid=Q11435648|1=Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki}}'' and ``Ruiju Zeikensho'' {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|pp=48-49}}. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Kenji|Q1327993|qid=Q1327993|1=Kenji (era)}} ({{ill|lt=1275|Q5529|qid=Q5529|1=1275}}), (Nara City)|Q1203935|Eison|Q11410722}} of Saidaiji Temple led a pilgrimage of over 100 monks, and {{jalink|Mongol Invasion|Q208156|Mongol Invasion] {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{Sfn|Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries|2000|pp=48-49}}, which is translating and praising the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Great Pannya Sutra|1=UNKNOWN}}. [[File:Hideyori Toyotomi.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|lt=Hideyori Toyotomi|Q313641|qid=Q313641|1=Toyotomi Hideyori}}}}]]In {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|qid=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|qid=Q6680|1=1574}}), a battle broke out between the priest Aritada Mizuhashiri and {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|qid=Q171411|1=Oda Nobunaga}}, and the shrine building was burned down {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. The main shrine was rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyori|Q313641|qid=Q313641|1=Toyotomi Hideyori}} in {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|qid=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 7th year ({{ill|lt=1602|Q6727|qid=Q6727|1=1602}}), Daimyojin Bridge was rebuilt in November (the imitation jewel still exists), and in 8th year of Keicho ({{ill|lt=1603|Q6735|qid=Q6735|1=1603}}) In November, a fishing lantern was donated (still in existence), and in the 10th year of the Keicho era ({{ill|lt=1605|Q6741|qid=Q6741|1=1605}}), the shrine territory and Yamanari were donated for restoration {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. In October of the 19th year of Keicho ({{ill|lt=1614|Q6781|qid=Q6781|1=1614}}), there was a So, {{ill|lt=Katsushige Itakura|Q771609|qid=Q771609|1=Itakura Katsushige}} of {{ill|lt=Kyoto Shoshidai|Q855977|qid=Q855977|1=Kyoto Shoshidai}} has issued three prohibited articles {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.In {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, 5 koku 9 to 5 sho were donated as shrine territory from Toyoura village {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine 4-za (shikinai shrine)|1979}}. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Keian|Q1146135|qid=Q1146135|1=Keian}} ({{ill|lt=1648|Q6941|qid=Q6941|1=1648}}), {{ill|lt=shuinjo|Q11093673|qid=Q11093673|1=shuinjō}} prohibited the felling of forests and bamboo trees and exempted them from various duties.{{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Throughout the Edo period, it was not revered by the {{ill|lt=Edo shogunate|Q205662|qid=Q205662|lt=shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate}}, but the current main shrine was built by parishioners in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|qid=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}}9 ({{ill|lt=1826|Q7004|qid=Q7004|1=1826}}). ==== Modern times and later ==== After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|qid=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in May of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}4 ({{ill|lt=1871|Q7746|qid=Q7746|1=1871}}) |Modern shrine rank system|Q712534}} ranked as {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system#government shrine|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=Kanpei Taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine 4th seat (shikinai shrine)|1979}}. After the war, in {{ill|lt=1948|Q5165|qid=Q5165|1=1948}} ({{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}23), it was added to the {{ill|lt=Beppyote Shrine|Q10898274|qid=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Shrine Honcho|Q908077|qid=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. === God level === * Record of divine investiture to Amagoyane no Mikoto {{Sfn|Shinto/Shrine Historical Materials Collection}} ** May 9, {{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|qid=Q1075605|lt=Jowa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=836|Q24957|qid=Q24957|1=836}}), from Junior Third Rank, Third Rank to Seisan Rank, Third Class ("{{ill|lt=Shoku Nippon Ki|Q1041301|qid=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}") "Shoku Nihon Koki" May Chomi (9th), Jowa 3rd year (836) (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The notation is "Amagoyane no Mikoto". ** On October 29, 1986 ({{ill|lt=839|Q23874|qid=Q23874|1=839}}), he was promoted from Seisan Rank 2nd Class {{sub|Mama}} to Junior 2nd Rank 2nd Class {{sub|Mama}} ("Shoku Nihon Koki") "Shoku Nihon Koki" October 29th, Jowa 6 (839) article (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The notation is "Amagoyane no Mikoto". ** As of June 8, 10th year of the Jowa era ({{ill|lt=843|Q23469|qid=Q23469|1=843}}, Junior Second Rank, Third Rank ("Shoku Nihon Koki") "Shoku Nihon Koki" Article on June 8th, 10th year of Jowa (843) (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The name is "Hiraoka Daijinja Shrine".** Reference: September 15, {{ill|lt=Kasho|Q1373157|qid=Q1373157|1=Kashō}}3 years ({{ill|lt=850 years|Q30364|qid=Q30364|1=850}}), Juichii (``{{ill|lt=Japanese Emperor Montoku Annals|Q1052905|qid=Q1052905|1=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku}}'') ``Japanese Emperor Montoku Annals'' Article on September 15th, Kasho 3rd year (850). **: {{Small|Note) In this article, the four deities enshrined at Kasuga Shrine: Takemiga Mamechi no Mikoto, Ihabisu no Mikoto, Amagoyane no Mikoto, and Hime no Kami are elevated to the divine rank. There is a theory that Motosha and Kasugasha were ordained at the same time ``Kasuga Taisha'' ``Japanese Historical Place Names 30 Place Names of Nara Prefecture'' Heibonsha, 1981. . }} ** As of October 19, {{ill|lt=Narihira|Q1324664|qid=Q1324664|1=Saikō}}3 years ({{ill|lt=856 years|Q30370|qid=Q30370|1=856}}), Juichii (``Japanese Emperor Montoku's Annals'') Article on October 19th of the 19th year of the 3rd year of Saihira (856) in the Annals of Emperor Montoku of Japan (see {{Harvnb|Collection of Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials}}). - The spelling is "Hiraokagami".** {{ill|lt=Cheonan (Japan)|Q1075611|qid=Q1075611|lt=Cheonan|1=Ten'an}} January 27, 3rd year ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|qid=Q23897|1=859}}), from Junior 1st Rank, 3rd Class to Seiichii, 3rd Class ("{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jiroki|Q1052981|qid=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}") "Japan's Sandai Jitsuroku" New Year's 27th Article, Jogan 1st year (859) (see {{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection}}). - The notation is "Hiraoka Amagoyane no Mikoto". * Record of divine investiture to Hime-kami {{Sfn|Collection of Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials}} ** May 9, 836, from the lower junior fourth rank to the upper junior fourth rank (Shoku Nihon Koki) - Written as "Hime-kami". **October 29, 839, Ju4i Upper to Sho4 Lower ("Shoku Nihon Koki") - Written as "Hime God". **Reference: September 15, 850, Shoshii 1st (``Nihon Montoku Tenno Jirroku'') ``Nihon Montoku Tenno Jirroku,'' September 15th, Kasho 3 (850) article. **: {{Small|Note) Same as above. }} ** On January 27, 859, from the 6th rank of Sho4i (Junior Rank) to the 6th rank of Jusani (Junior Sandai Jitsuroku) - The name is written as "Hiraoka Himejin". === Shinto priest === The priesthood of Hiraoka Shrine is said to have been held by the ``Hiraoka clan'' (Hiraoka clan) in {{ill|lt=ancient|Q41493|qid=Q41493|1=Ancient history}}. The Hiraoka clan is one of the Nakatomi clans, and is listed in "{{ill|lt=Shinsen Sōjiroku|Q1045362|qid=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}" "Shinsen Sōjiroku" Kamibetsu Kawachi Province Hiraoka Renjo. says that he is a descendant of Taimi Omi, the 14th grandson of Tsuhaya Tamami no Mikoto. In the same record, many of the Nakatomi clans in Kawachi Province are considered to be the descendants of Tsuhayadama no Mikoto (the ancestor of Amagoyane no Mikoto), and their character is different from that of the main sect of the Nakatomi clan, which is the descendant of Amagoyane no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Hikaru Matsuo|2014}}.In national history, in the 10th year of the Jowa era (843) , it is said that the priests of Hiraoka Daijinja Shrine and others were entrusted with the scepter, and in the 7th year of the Jogan era (865) , it appears that one ``Hiraoka Shinto Priest'' was provided with silk threads for the spring and winter colors, but it is assumed that the Hiraoka Ren was in charge of these Shinto Priests {{Sfn|Hiraoka (Ancient Clan Encyclopedia)|2015}}. In addition, the official organization is the ``Engishiki'' , there are descriptions of Shinto priests, Monoki, Negi, Shuku, Dankin, flute makers, 2 Urabes, and 8 Zenbes, but {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|pp=48-49}}, "{{ill|lt=Ruijufu Sensho|Q11665188|qid=Q11665188|1=Ruijufusenshō}}" "Ruijufusensho" Daijokanfu dated May 11, 952. According to and others, the priest, Monoki, seems to have been appointed by {{ill|lt=Onakatomi clan|Q11431853|qid=Q11431853|1=Ōnakatomi clan}} (Hiraoka clan) {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|pp=48-49}}. {{ill|lt=Kangen|Q1194187|qid=Q1194187|1=Kangen}}3 year ({{ill|lt=1245|Q5440|qid=Q5440|1=1245}}) document "Hiradoki" Heikei Takashomon dated New Year 29, Kangen 3 year (1245). According to , since the appointment of Kimeifu at the time of {{ill|lt=Emperor Shirakawa|Q60949|qid=Q60949|lt=Shirakawain|1=Emperor Shirakawa}}, people other than the Hiraoka clan have been appointed to the priesthood position, and even within the Hiraoka clan, the Tametomo style has continued. It is said that the Yoriyasu school divided into two groups and fought against each other.Since the Middle Ages, the '''{{ill|lt=Mizuhashi clan|Q11549239|qid=Q11549239|1=Mizuhashi clan}}''' (Mizuhayashi) was the chief priest, and the Torii clan was the chief priest. The Mizuhashi clan is a clan with the Fujiwara surname that claims to be descendants of the Hiraoka clan, and grew from a priest to a feudal lord (territorial lord) in the medieval period by inheriting the positions of social affairs, head priest, and head priest.{{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|pp=48-49}}. Although it declined during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, it continued as a Shinto priest at Hiraoka Shrine until the end of the Edo period {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{Sfn|Hikaru Matsuo|2014}}. === Shrine building construction === {{ill|lt=Tenki|Q1193591|qid=Q1193591|1=Tengi}}4 ({{ja link|1056 years|Q19375}})・{{ill|lt=Kanji|Q1193874|qid=Q1193874|1=Kanji (era)}}5 years ({{ill|lt=1091 years|Q19468|qid=Q19468|1=1091}})・{ {jalink|Hoji|Q1327400}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=1247|Q5482|qid=Q5482|1=1247}}), {{ill|lt=Motonoku|Q1327385|qid=Q1327385|1=Gentoku}}2nd year It is said that in ({{ill|lt=1330|Q5996|qid=Q5996|1=1330}}), shrine buildings were burnt down and shrines were transferred.{{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|pp=48-49}}. According to the same book, it was in decline during the Muromachi period, but was revived by shrine parishioners in {{ill|lt=Bunmei (Japan)|Q1193544|qid=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei|1=Bunmei}} ({{ill|lt=1475|Q6608|qid=Q6608|1=1475}}) {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|pp=48-49}}.In {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|qid=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}7 ({{ill|lt=1579|Q6709|qid=Q6709|1=1579}}), {{jalink|Oda Nobunaga|Q171411} The main shrine and 17 shrines and subsidiary shrines were destroyed by fire during the 1979 war. After that, it was rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyori|Q313641|qid=Q313641|1=Toyotomi Hideyori}} in {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|qid=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 7th year ({{ill|lt=1602|Q6727|qid=Q6727|1=1602}}). An illustration from 2012 ({{ill|lt=1656|Q6982|qid=Q6982|1=1656}}) depicts the precincts and temple tower at the time of Hideyori's reconstruction. Going down from there, the current main shrine was built in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|qid=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}}9 ({{ill|lt=1826|Q7004|qid=Q7004|1=1826}}) of the Edo period. In addition, there were six {{ill|lt=Jinguji|Q1074971|qid=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}} temples: Jingoji, Genkoan, Hiraokaji, Horenan, Raigoji, and Shindoji.{{Sfn|Hiraoka Four shrines (Shikinaisha)|1979}}{{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|pp=48-49}}. Jingo-ji Temple and Hiraoka-ji Temple are said to date back to the Middle Ages {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|pp=48-49}}. === Precincts === [[File:Hiraoka-jinja, honden-1.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main shrine/transparent wall}}]] [[File:Hiraoka-jinja haiden2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Shrine}}]] * Main shrine - Consists of 4 buildings. In the shrine precinct surrounded by the central gate and transparent wall, the second hall (Hime no Mikoto), first hall (Amagoyane no Mikoto), fourth hall (Takemikazuchi no Mikoto), and third hall (Ketsunushi no Mikoto) are lined up in a line from south to north in the north-south direction, directly facing the approach. The style of each hall is Hiraoka-zukuri, and the roof is {{ill|lt=cypress bark|Q90373477|qid=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}}, and especially the first and second halls have a lot of old materials left. It looks similar to the main hall of Kasuga Taisha Shrine, but in the case of the main hall, the four buildings are independent. Although it does not maintain the traditions of ancient times, it is said to be a building that clearly shows the characteristics of the late to late Edo period with its tomata, rainbow beams, and wooden nose. {{Sfn|Higashiosaka City Designated Cultural Properties Revised Edition|2012}}. In addition, the shrine was constructed from the Heisei period to the Reiwa period, and restoration work was carried out to make it look like the shrine building from the late Edo period, which is confirmed in existing historical materials, so it has a unique form of the shrine building, which is Kasuga Shirenmaioka-zukuri, which is different from Kasuga-zukuri. ** First Hall (Tangible Cultural Property designated by Higashiosaka City) - Rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|qid=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}}9 ({{ill|lt=1826|Q7004|qid=Q7004|1=1826}}).** Second Hall (Tangible Cultural Property designated by Higashiosaka City) - Rebuilt in 1826. ** Third Hall (Tangible Cultural Property designated by Higashiosaka City) - Rebuilt in 1826. ** Fourth Hall (Tangible Cultural Property designated by Higashiosaka City) - Rebuilt in 1826. * Middle gate * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1879|Q7783|qid=Q7783|1=1879}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}12). was built according to the status of the former Kanpei Taisha Shrine. The roof is made of copper sheets (formerly cypress bark), and the plaque ``Hiraoka Shrine'' is written by {{ill|lt=Memi Sanjo|Q516646|qid=Q516646|1=Sanjō Sanetomi}}. * Gokaden * Keimeiden (shrine office) * Gathering place * Saikan - Built in {{ill|lt=1936|Q18649|qid=Q18649|1=1936}} ({{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}11) as a shrine office. It is currently used as a funeral hall. .* Hiraoka Bairin (designated by Higashiosaka City as a scenic spot {{ill|lt=scenic spot|Q11414752|qid=Q11414752|1=Place of Scenic Beauty}}) - The place where the {{ill|lt=Obaku sect|Q384728|qid=Q384728|1=Ōbaku}} Jingoji Temple of Hiraoka Shrine was once located. {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|qid=Q7757|1=1873}} (Meiji 6) After Jingo-ji Temple was abandoned, volunteers from Toyoura village planted plum trees and set up a viewing platform on the site. {{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|qid=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}In the first year, it was developed and expanded by Osaka businessmen and Daiki no Shokokai, and in the early Showa period, by the Shinen Hoshokai.{{Sfn|Designated Cultural Property of Higashiosaka City Revised Edition|2012}} * Kozudake Main Shrine - The place that is said to be the founding place of our company ({{Coord|34|40|4.56|N|135|39|25.78|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|name=Kozudake Main Shrine}}), located on the east mountain top of the current shrine grounds. It was once a forbidden area, but a stone monument was built in {{ill|lt=1981|Q2437|qid=Q2437|1=1981}} (1981), and a stone shrine was built in {{ill|lt=1993|Q2065|qid=Q2065|1=1993}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|qid=Q234510|1=Heisei era}}5), and it remains today.The Ichi-no-Torii ({{Coord|34|40|7.64|N|135|38|29.39|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|name=Hiraoka Shrine Ichi-no-Torii}}) is built along {{ill|lt=Higashi Koya Kaido|Q11528777|qid=Q11528777|1=Higashi Kōya Kaidō}} about 800 meters west of the approach from the Nino-Torii. group="note">By the way, the name of the place where the first torii gate was built is Torii Town. . Kozudake can be seen in the front and back, and the approach to the shrine is called ``Matsu no Baba.'' The nearby stone lantern was once built in the center of the torii gate, and is the oldest in Higashiosaka city with an inscription dating back to {{ill|lt=Jokyo|Q1146221|qid=Q1146221|1=Jōkyō}}2 ({{ill|lt=1685|Q7660|qid=Q7660|1=1685}}). The Ichino Torii and Matsu no Baba are also depicted in the Kawachi-kuni Hosomizu of 1776|Q7703}} ({{ill|lt=Yasunaga (era name)|Q1193910|qid=Q1193910|lt=Yasunaga|1=An'ei}}5 ({{ill|lt=1776|Q7703|qid=Q7703|1=1776}})Hiraoka Shrine Ichino Torii Historic Site Explanation Board (Higashiosaka City Board of Education, installed in 1991). . {{small|Kozudake can be seen in the background. }} === Setsusha === [[File:Hiraoka-jinja, Wakamiya-sha.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Wakamiyasha}}]] There are two Setsusha shrines, one Setsusha and one Setsusha (both are shrines within the precincts) . '''Setsusha''' * Wakamiyasha ** Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Tenshi Unne no Mikoto|Q91088537|qid=Q91088537|1=Ame-no-Oshikumone}} ** Annual festival: {{ill|lt=December 17th|Q2413|qid=Q2413|1=December 17}} *: The enshrined deity, Amenoosikumone, is the {{ill|lt=Mikogami|Q352176|qid=Q352176|1=Mikogami}} of Amagoyane no Mikoto. It is also enshrined as a Wakamiya shrine at Kasuga Taisha Shrine, etc.. Although it does not appear in "{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|qid=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}" or "{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|qid=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}," in "{{ill|lt=Nakatomi Juji|Q24877078|qid=Q24877078|1=Nakatomi no Yogoto}}" there is a legend that says that he climbed to the top of the heavens and took the water for the imperial grandchild.{{ill|lt=Masaaki Ueda|Q3297094|qid=Q3297094|1=Masaaki Ueda}} "New Research on Ancient Japan and East Asia" Fujiwara Shoten, 2015, pp. 108-114. . There is a well called ``Izumoi'' in the back of the shrine building (one theory is the origin of the place name Izumoi-cho) . '''Suesha''' * Tenjin Jigisha ** Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Amatsukami/Kunitsukami|Q11442793|qid=Q11442793|1=Amatsukami and Kunitsukami}} ** Annual festival: {{ill|lt=March 25th|Q2454|qid=Q2454|1=March 25}}*: When organizing the shrines in {{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|qid=Q7753|1=1872}} (Meiji 5), 19 subsidiary shrines in the precincts (or 15 subsidiary shrines {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine 4-za (Shikiuchi-sha) |1979}}) and more than 20 nearby shrines {{Sfn|Hiraoka-jinja 4-za (Shikiuchi-sha)|1979}} are enshrined together. Some of them have since been re-enshrined after canceling their joint enshrinement. File:Hiraoka-jinja, Tenjinchigi-sha.jpg|Tenjin Jigisha === Festival === === Annual festival === {{hidden begin |title = List of annual festivals |border = #aaa solid 1px |titlestyle = text-align: center; ; background:lightgrey; }} List of festivals held annually at Hiraoka Shrine [http://www.hiraoka-jinja.org/rituals/index.html Shinto rituals and festivals] (official website). . * Monthly ** One Day Festival (Monthly Festival) (1st of every month) - Excluding January, February, and March. Small festival ceremony. * January ** Saitan Festival ({{ill|lt=January 1st|Q2150|qid=Q2150|1=January 1}}) - Middle festival ceremony. ** Two-day festival ({{ill|lt=January 2nd|Q2151|qid=Q2151|1=January 2}}) - Small festival ceremony. ** Genshi Festival ({{ill|lt=January 3rd|Q2156|qid=Q2156|1=January 3}}) - Middle Festival Ceremony. ** '''Glue fortune-telling ritual'' ({{ill|lt=January 11th|Q2247|qid=Q2247|1=January 11}}) - Special ritual. Osaka Prefecture {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|1=UNKNOWN}}. (Currently only on January 15th) ** Congee Fortune Offering Festival ({{ill|lt=January 15th|Q2260|qid=Q2260|1=January 15}}) - Middle festival ceremony. *February ** '''Regular Festival''' ({{ill|lt=February 1st|Q2312|qid=Q2312|1=February 1}}) - Grand Festival. ** Setsubun Festival ({{ill|lt=Setsubun|Q746798|qid=Q746798|lt=Setsubun Day|1=Setsubun}}) - Small festival ceremony. ** Kigen Festival ({{ill|lt=February 11th|Q2335|qid=Q2335|1=February 11}}) - Middle festival ceremony.** '''Prayer Year Festival'' ({{ill|lt=February 17th|Q2341|qid=Q2341|1=February 17}}) - Grand festival. * March ** Plum Blossom Festival ({{ill|lt=March 1st|Q2393|qid=Q2393|1=March 1}}) - Small festival ceremony. ** Vernal Equinox Festival ({{ill|lt=Vernal Equinox Day|Q1049141|qid=Q1049141|1=Vernal Equinox Day}}) - Distant Worship Ceremony. ** Suesha Tenjin Ji Gisha annual festival ({{ill|lt=March 25th|Q2454|qid=Q2454|1=March 25}}) - Middle festival ceremony. *April ** Cherry Blossom Festival ({{ill|lt=April 3rd|Q2509|qid=Q2509|1=April 3}}) - Small festival ceremony. * May ** Thanksgiving ({{ill|lt=May 4th|Q2549|qid=Q2549|1=May 4}}) - Small festival ceremony. ** Kodaiko Festival ({{ill|lt=May 5th|Q2550|qid=Q2550|1=May 5}}) - A divine event. ** Heikoku Festival ({{ill|lt=May 21st|Q2580|qid=Q2580|1=May 21}}) - Small festival ceremony. * June ** '''Summer Purification''' ({{ill|lt=June 30th|Q2657|qid=Q2657|1=June 30}}) - Grand ritual. *August ** Peace Memorial Festival ({{ill|lt=August 15|Q2774|qid=Q2774|1=August 15}}) - Small festival ceremony. ** Hiraoka Tomei Festival (4th Sunday in August) - Small festival ceremony. ** Fuchin Prayer Festival ({{ill|lt=August 25th|Q2819|qid=Q2819|1=August 25}}) - Small festival ceremony. *September ** Health and Longevity Prayer Festival ({{ill|lt=Respect for the Aged Day|Q1186127|qid=Q1186127|1=Respect for the Aged Day}}) - Small festival ceremony. (Currently not done) ** Kozudake Hongu Shrine Festival ({{ill|lt=September 16th|Q2897|qid=Q2897|1=September 16}}) - Middle Festival Ceremony. ** Autumn Equinox Festival ({{ill|lt=Autumn Equinox Day|Q1186410|qid=Q1186410|1=Autumnal Equinox Day}}) - Distant Worship Ceremony. ** Fuchin Hosaisai ({{ill|lt=September 25th|Q2886|qid=Q2886|1=September 25}}) - Small festival ceremony.* October ** Akigo Festival Yoimiya Festival ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Saturday and Sunday before Sports Day|1=UNKNOWN}}) - Small festival ceremony. ** '''Akigo Festival'' ({{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|qid=Q2919|1=October 15}}) - Grand festival ceremony. ** Kaminasai Day Festival ({{ill|lt=October 17th|Q2962|qid=Q2962|1=October 17}}) - Middle Festival Ceremony. *November ** Meiji Festival ({{ill|lt=November 3rd|Q2995|qid=Q2995|1=November 3}}) - Middle festival ceremony. ** '''Ninamesai''' ({{ill|lt=November 23rd|Q3021|qid=Q3021|1=November 23}}) - Grand festival. ** Shichigosan event (during November) *December ** Joshin Festival (First Monkey Day) - Small festival ceremony. ** Setsusha Wakamiya Shrine Annual Festival ({{ill|lt=December 17th|Q2413|qid=Q2413|1=December 17}}) - Middle Festival Ceremony. ** Tencho Festival ({{ill|lt=December 23rd|Q2676|qid=Q2676|1=December 23}}) ** '''Shimonakake Shinto Ritual (Comedy Shinto Ritual)''' (December 23rd) - Special Shinto Ritual. Intangible folk cultural property designated by Higashiosaka City. ** '''December 31st | Q2912}}' - Grand ritual. ** New Year's Eve Festival (December 31st) - Small festival ceremony. {{hidden end}} === Special ritual === ; {{ill|lt=porridge fortune-telling|Q11604655|qid=Q11604655|1=Kayu ura}} Shinto ritual : "Kayuura Shiji". {{ill|lt=January 11th|Q2247|qid=Q2247|lt=January 15th|1=January 11}}. : {{ill|lt=Little New Year|Q137803901|qid=Q137803901|1=koshōgatsu}} yearly fortune telling ritual. Nowadays, due to advances in printing technology, the ritual is held on January 15th and the results are announced afterwards. In the ritual, 5 square meters of rice and 3 square meters of red beans are boiled in a large pot, and a bundle of 53 fortune-telling bamboos is hung inside the pot, and the red bean porridge inside the bamboo is used to predict the year's bountiful harvest. In addition, 12 black oak fortune-telling trees (13 in leap years) are placed in the stove, and the weather is predicted by the degree of charring of the trees. The Shinto ritual is also described in "Kawachi Kanmeishoki" {{Sfn|Hiraoka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Selected as an Osaka Prefecture {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn|Designated Cultural Property of Higashiosaka City Revised Edition|2012}}. ; Shimenawakake ritual (comedy ritual) : "Shimekake Shinji". {{ill|lt=December 23|Q2676|qid=Q2676|1=December 23}}. : A ritual that invites spring through laughter. It used to be held on {{ill|lt=January 8|Q2234|qid=Q2234|1=January 8}} before the porridge divine ritual. During the ritual, a large shimenawa rope is strung around a shimenawa pillar at the bottom of the stone steps, and the priests and parishioners laugh loudly under the shimenawa pillar, praying for the arrival of spring. It has been designated as an intangible folk cultural property designated by Higashiosaka City {{Sfn|Higashiosaka City Designated Cultural Property Revised Edition|2012}}. === Cultural assets === === Osaka Prefecture Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * Congee fortune-telling ritual at Hiraoka Shrine (attached: Hiraoka Shrine Congee Diary Volume 1) - Selected on August 4, 1978 {{Sfn|Designated Cultural Property of Higashiosaka City Revised Edition|2012}}[http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai/hunai-siteiichiran.html Osaka Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties List] (Osaka Prefecture homepage). . === Cultural property designated by Higashiosaka City === *Tangible cultural property ** Main shrine 4 buildings (buildings) **: Constructed in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|qid=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}}9 ({{ill|lt=1826|Q7004|qid=Q7004|1=1826}}) during the Edo period. It was designated on March 25, 1974, and eight bronze hanging lanterns were additionally designated on May 11, 1995. ** Hiraoka Shrine copper imitation jewel (craft) **: Created in the early Edo period, {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|qid=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}7 ({{ill|lt=1602|Q6727|qid=Q6727|1=1602}}). This is a wooden bridge imitation jewel donated by Toyotomi Hideyori. It measures 36.5 centimeters in height and has an inscription engraved on its body. Designated on March 25, 1974 {{Sfn|Designated Cultural Property of Higashiosaka City Revised Edition|2012}}. ** Hiraoka Shrine copper hanging lantern (craft) **: Created in the early Edo period, Keicho 8 ({{ill|lt=1603|Q6735|qid=Q6735|1=1603}}). Donation by Hideyori Toyotomi. It is hexagonal and measures 48.5 centimeters in height, and inscriptions are engraved on four sides of the firebox's pillars. Designated on March 25, 1974 {{Sfn|Designated Cultural Property of Higashiosaka City Revised Edition|2012}}. ** Colored illustration of Hiraoka Shrine on silk (painting)**: Written by Kano Hachirobei in the 2nd year of {{ill|lt=Meireki|Q1192766|qid=Q1192766|1=Meireki}} ({{ill|lt=1656|Q6982|qid=Q6982|1=1656}}) during the Edo period. Height: 196.5 cm, Width: 88.5 cm. There is a description of the background of the painting at the bottom left of the screen, which says that it is a drawing depicting the precincts as they were before they fell into disrepair in the 2nd year of Meireki. Designated on March 25, 1974 {{Sfn|Designated Cultural Property of Higashiosaka City Revised Edition|2012}}. * Intangible folk cultural assets ** Shimenawakake ritual at Hiraoka Shrine - Designated on March 25, 1974 {{Sfn|Designated Cultural Property of Higashiosaka City Revised Edition|2012}}. * Scenic spots ** Hiraoka Bairin - Designated on March 25, 1974 {{Sfn|Higashiosaka City Designated Cultural Property Revised Edition|2012}}. === Others === The {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=juniper|1=UNKNOWN}} on the temple grounds was once designated as a natural monument by Osaka Prefecture in 1938 (Showa 13), but it was damaged by {{ill|lt=Second Muroto Typhoon|Q1140009|qid=Q1140009|1=Typhoon Nancy (1961)}} in {{ill|lt=1961|Q3696|qid=Q3696|1=1961}} (Showa 36) and was cut down in the 1960s.. ===
